category.label: Integration and Accumulation of Change
category.description: Develops the definite integral as accumulated change, connects differentiation to antidifferentiation via the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, and builds procedural fluency with basic antiderivative rules, u-substitution, and algebraic rewriting techniques for integrands.
category.yield_rationale: Highest-weighted unit (17–20% of exam); FTC and u-sub appear as sub-parts in nearly every accumulation, area, and DE FRQ; foundational for Units 7 and 8.

umbrellas.label: Accumulation of Change and Signed Area
umbrellas.description: Interprets the definite integral as accumulated change; connects the area under a rate curve to net change; distinguishes positive and negative contributions to a signed area.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Conceptual foundation tested in MC and accumulation FRQs; unit weighting 17–20% but this topic contributes via interpretation sub-parts rather than standalone questions.

in_depth.label: Net Change as a Definite Integral
in_depth.description: Recognizes that ∫ₐᵇ r(t) dt gives the net change in a quantity when r is a rate; identifies the integral as accumulated change rather than area in applied contexts.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Net-change interpretation appears in every accumulation FRQ and is required to write correct integral expressions in context.

in_depth.label: Signed Area and Negative Contributions
in_depth.description: Determines when a definite integral is negative (integrand below x-axis) and interprets the signed value as net rather than total accumulation; distinguishes signed from unsigned area.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Signed-area reasoning appears in MC every year and is prerequisite for total-distance problems in Unit 8.

in_depth.label: Distinguishing a Definite Integral (Number) from an Indefinite Integral (Family of Functions)
in_depth.description: Explains that ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx evaluates to a single real number (the net signed area), while ∫f(x) dx produces a family of antiderivatives differing by a constant; recognizes notation cues that distinguish the two forms.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual distinction between definite and indefinite integrals is tested in MC identification questions and underpins correct FRQ communication; belongs in Unit 6's mathematical scope rather than the applied-context scope owned by Unit 8.

in_depth.label: Distinguishing Area from Accumulation When f Is Negative
in_depth.description: Computes or interprets ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx when f dips below the x-axis; correctly identifies that the integral subtracts the below-axis region rather than adding it.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual MC question type; supports the graphical analysis of accumulation functions in topic 6.5.

umbrellas.label: Riemann Sum Approximations
umbrellas.description: Approximates definite integrals using left, right, midpoint, and trapezoidal sums from tables or graphs; assesses whether each approximation is an over- or under-estimate based on function behavior.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Riemann sum from a table of values is a near-annual FRQ sub-part (2023 Q1, 2021 Q1); over/under-estimate analysis is explicitly tested in both MC and FRQ.

in_depth.label: Left and Right Riemann Sum from a Table
in_depth.description: Computes a left or right Riemann sum with specified subintervals from a table of function values; correctly multiplies each function value by the corresponding subinterval width.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The most common Riemann sum FRQ sub-task; appears explicitly in Rate/Data archetype FRQs most years.

in_depth.label: Midpoint Riemann Sum
in_depth.description: Evaluates a midpoint Riemann sum by using function values at the midpoint of each subinterval; distinguishes midpoint from left/right endpoint selection.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Midpoint sum appears in MC; less common than left/right in FRQ but tested in MC Part A.

in_depth.label: Trapezoidal Approximation
in_depth.description: Computes a trapezoidal sum T = Σ[(f(xᵢ) + f(xᵢ₊₁))/2 · Δx] from a table; interprets trapezoidal sum as the average of left and right Riemann sums on uniform partitions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Trapezoidal approximation from a table is an explicit FRQ sub-part in the Rate/Data archetype and appears in MC most years.

in_depth.label: Over- and Under-Estimate Analysis for Riemann Sums
in_depth.description: Determines whether a left, right, or trapezoidal sum over- or under-estimates the true integral by examining whether f is increasing, decreasing, concave up, or concave down on the interval.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Over/under-estimate justification is a required written justification in FRQ and a frequent MC distractor; tests both Riemann sums and function behavior together.

in_depth.label: Riemann Sums with Unequal Subintervals
in_depth.description: Handles Riemann sum calculations when subinterval widths are not equal; multiplies each function value by its specific Δxᵢ from the given partition.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Exam tables routinely use unequal subintervals; failure to use correct widths is the primary distractor error on these problems.

umbrellas.label: Riemann Sums, Summation Notation, and Integral Notation
umbrellas.description: Connects the limit of a Riemann sum to the definite integral symbol; interprets ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx as a limit of sums, reads integral notation fluently, and writes correct integral expressions for described quantities.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Integral notation fluency is required to write correct integral expressions in FRQ; MC tests recognition of integrals as limits of Riemann sums.

in_depth.label: Connecting Riemann Sum Notation to Integral Notation
in_depth.description: Recognizes that lim_{n→∞} Σf(xᵢ)Δx = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx; identifies the integrand, limits of integration, and differential dx from a given Riemann sum expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Limit-of-sums to integral connection appears in MC as a matching or identification question; conceptually essential for the FTC derivation.

in_depth.label: Reading and Writing Integral Notation
in_depth.description: Correctly interprets and writes ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx notation including the integrand, differential, and limits; identifies the variable of integration and distinguishes it from parameters.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Notation fluency is tested implicitly on every FRQ requiring an integral expression; MC tests correct notation in 'which expression represents...' questions.

in_depth.label: Writing a Definite Integral Expression for a Described Quantity
in_depth.description: Translates a verbal description (e.g., 'total amount of water added from t = 1 to t = 5') into the correct integral expression, identifying the integrand function and limits from context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Writing an integral expression is a standard FRQ sub-task in the Rate/Data and Accumulation archetypes; earns explicit points.

in_depth.label: Summation (Σ) Notation for Riemann Sums
in_depth.description: Reads and evaluates Σ notation for finite sums; writes a Riemann sum in Σ form specifying the index, function value, and width; connects the finite sum to the integral in the limit.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Σ notation appears in MC as a Riemann sum representation; less frequently tested than the integral notation itself.

umbrellas.label: FTC Part 1: Accumulation Functions and Their Derivatives
umbrellas.description: Differentiates F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt using the Fundamental Theorem Part 1 in its basic form (constant lower limit, upper limit = x); handles the variable-lower-limit orientation flip; analyzes sign and behavior of F using the sign of f. Chain-rule extension (variable upper limit g(x)) is scoped to Unit 8.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: FTC Part 1 is one of the highest-frequency skills on the exam; appears in FRQ every year (2021 Q4, 2025 Q4) and constantly in MC. Yield reflects the basic and orientation-flip cases owned here; the chain-rule extension is consolidated in Unit 8 to avoid double-counting.

in_depth.label: FTC Part 1: d/dx of ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt
in_depth.description: Applies d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t) dt] = f(x) directly; recognizes that the derivative of an accumulation function with variable upper limit equals the integrand evaluated at the upper limit.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The most directly tested FTC statement; appears in MC every year and is Step 1 of the FTC FRQ archetype.

in_depth.label: FTC Part 1: Basic Case (Constant Lower Limit, Upper Limit = x) with and without an Initial Condition
in_depth.description: Applies d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t) dt] = f(x) in the standard case where the lower limit is a constant and the upper limit is exactly x; distinguishes this from the chain-rule extension; uses an initial condition F(a) = k when given to identify a specific antiderivative.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The basic FTC1 case (no chain rule) is the entry-level skill for accumulation function derivatives; initial-condition sub-parts appear in FRQ annually and must be clearly separated from the chain-rule variant owned by Unit 8.

in_depth.label: FTC Part 1 with Variable Lower Limit
in_depth.description: Handles d/dx[∫ₓᵇ f(t) dt] = −f(x) by reversing the bounds first; applies the negative sign from interval reversal before differentiating.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Variable-lower-limit variant tests bounds reversal plus FTC1; appears in MC as a trap for students who ignore the orientation.

in_depth.label: Second Derivative of an Accumulation Function
in_depth.description: Computes F''(x) by differentiating f(x) = F'(x); uses F'' = f' to locate inflection points of F and determine concavity of F from the graph of f.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Accumulation FRQ archetype asks for F''(x) to determine concavity/inflection of F; tested annually.

umbrellas.label: Behavior of Accumulation Functions from a Graph of f
umbrellas.description: Analyzes F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt graphically: determines where F is increasing or decreasing, identifies horizontal tangents, sketches a rough graph of F, and reasons about the sign and relative comparisons of F from the graph of f. Extrema identification and numeric evaluation of F are scoped to Unit 8.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Accumulation function graphical analysis is near-annual in FRQ and MC; yield reflects qualitative and sign-based reasoning owned here. Numeric extrema and F-value computation are consolidated in Unit 8 (CED 8.2/8.3) to avoid double-counting.

in_depth.label: Determining Where F Is Increasing or Decreasing from the Graph of f
in_depth.description: Uses F'(x) = f(x) to conclude F is increasing where f > 0 and decreasing where f < 0; identifies intervals from a graph of f.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Increasing/decreasing analysis of F is the most common sub-task in the accumulation FRQ archetype; tested every year.

in_depth.label: Identifying Horizontal Tangents of F Where f(c) = 0
in_depth.description: Applies F'(c) = f(c) = 0 to conclude F has a horizontal tangent line at x = c; locates all x-values where the graph of f crosses or touches zero to identify horizontal tangents of F.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Recognizing that F'(c) = 0 iff f(c) = 0 is the conceptual link between zeros of f and horizontal tangents of F; tested in MC and required before extrema analysis in the accumulation FRQ archetype.

in_depth.label: Sketching a Rough Graph of F Given the Graph of f (Without Computing Exact Areas)
in_depth.description: Constructs a qualitative sketch of F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt from the graph of f by tracking where F rises/falls (sign of f), where it bends (sign of f'), and where it levels off (f = 0); does not require computing numeric area values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Qualitative sketching of an accumulation function from a graph of f is a graphical synthesis skill tested in MC and occasionally in FRQ; distinct from numeric evaluation owned by Unit 8.

in_depth.label: Comparing F(b) to F(a) Using the Sign of ∫f Without Numeric Evaluation
in_depth.description: Determines whether F(b) > F(a), F(b) < F(a), or F(b) = F(a) by examining whether ∫ₐᵇ f is positive, negative, or zero from the graph of f; avoids computing exact area values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign-based comparison of accumulated values is a conceptual MC question type; complements numeric evaluation owned by Unit 8 by focusing on qualitative reasoning.

in_depth.label: Identifying Where F Is Positive, Negative, or Zero from the Graph of f
in_depth.description: Determines the sign of F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt on an interval by tracking whether net accumulated area from a to x is positive, negative, or zero; reads geometric areas from the graph of f to reason about F's sign.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign-of-F reasoning from a graph of f is a conceptual accumulation skill tested in MC; requires integrating knowledge of signed area and FTC1 without numeric computation.

umbrellas.label: Properties of Definite Integrals
umbrellas.description: Applies linearity of integration (constant multiple, sum/difference), interval additivity (splitting/combining intervals), and interval reversal to evaluate or simplify definite integrals algebraically or from a graph/table.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Integral properties appear in MC every year; used to evaluate integrals from graphs or tables when direct computation is unavailable.

in_depth.label: Linearity of the Definite Integral (Constant Multiple and Sum)
in_depth.description: Applies ∫[cf(x) + g(x)]dx = c∫f(x)dx + ∫g(x)dx to split or combine integral expressions; uses known integral values to evaluate a new integral without direct computation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Constant-multiple and sum/difference properties appear in MC questions asking to evaluate an integral given others; high practical frequency.

in_depth.label: Splitting and Combining Intervals (Interval Additivity)
in_depth.description: Uses ∫ₐᶜ f = ∫ₐᵇ f + ∫ᵦᶜ f to split an integral at an interior point or to find a missing piece given two of the three integrals.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Interval-splitting is a frequent MC problem type and appears in FRQ when integrating piecewise or when a known partial integral is given.

in_depth.label: Reversing the Limits of Integration
in_depth.description: Applies ∫ₐᵇ f = −∫ᵦₐ f to flip bounds; correctly handles the sign change when reversing integration direction.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Bound reversal is tested directly in MC and is a prerequisite for FTC1 with a variable lower limit.

in_depth.label: Integral Over a Zero-Width Interval
in_depth.description: Recognizes that ∫ₐᵃ f(x) dx = 0 regardless of f(a); uses this property to simplify expressions or evaluate degenerate cases.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Zero-width integral appears occasionally in MC as a trap; lower exam frequency but important for completeness of properties.

in_depth.label: Evaluating Integrals Using Properties and a Graph
in_depth.description: Combines linearity, additivity, and reversal properties with area values read directly from a graph of f to evaluate a composite integral expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graph-based integral evaluation using properties is a standard MC question format; tests both property fluency and graph reading.

umbrellas.label: FTC Part 2: Evaluating Definite Integrals with Antiderivatives
umbrellas.description: Evaluates ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = F(b) − F(a) using the Fundamental Theorem Part 2; finds the appropriate antiderivative F, applies the evaluation at both bounds, and computes the net result.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: FTC Part 2 is the computational workhorse for every definite integral; used in virtually every integration FRQ and MC question.

in_depth.label: Applying the FTC Evaluation Rule F(b) − F(a)
in_depth.description: Finds an antiderivative F of f, evaluates F at both limits of integration, and computes F(b) − F(a); uses correct bracket notation [F(x)]ₐᵇ.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Core FTC2 application appears on every exam; used directly in FRQ and MC to complete definite integral evaluations.

in_depth.label: FTC Part 2 Applied to Trig and Exponential Integrands
in_depth.description: Evaluates definite integrals of sin, cos, e^x, and 1/x using their standard antiderivatives; applies boundary evaluation after each antiderivative is found.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Trig and exponential definite integrals appear regularly in MC and FRQ; requires both antiderivative recall and correct FTC2 execution.

in_depth.label: FTC Part 2 When U-Substitution Is Required Before Evaluating with Bounds
in_depth.description: Applies FTC Part 2 to a definite integral whose integrand is a product of a polynomial and a transcendental function (e.g., x·e^{x²}, x·cos(x²)); performs u-substitution first, converts the bounds to u-values, then evaluates F(b) − F(a) entirely in terms of u.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Definite integrals requiring u-sub before FTC2 evaluation are a high-frequency MC and FRQ sub-step; combining FTC2 with u-sub is the procedural capstone of Unit 6's integration technique scope.

in_depth.label: FTC Part 2 Applied to Polynomial Integrands
in_depth.description: Evaluates definite integrals of polynomial functions using the reverse power rule antiderivative; applies bounds and simplifies the resulting arithmetic.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Polynomial definite integrals are the most common computational integration task in no-calculator MC and FRQ.

umbrellas.label: Finding Antiderivatives and Basic Indefinite Integral Rules
umbrellas.description: Applies the reverse power rule and standard antiderivative formulas for trigonometric, exponential, and reciprocal functions to find indefinite integrals; includes the constant of integration +C.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Antiderivative computation appears in every integration FRQ and MC; foundational for all of Units 7 and 8.

in_depth.label: Reverse Power Rule for Antiderivatives
in_depth.description: Applies ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C for n ≠ −1; correctly increments the exponent and divides by the new exponent, including fractional and negative exponents.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reverse power rule is the single most-used antiderivative rule on the exam; appears in nearly every integration question.

in_depth.label: Antiderivatives of sin(x) and cos(x)
in_depth.description: Applies ∫sin(x) dx = −cos(x) + C and ∫cos(x) dx = sin(x) + C; recalls the sign conventions and uses them in both indefinite and definite integrals.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Trig antiderivatives appear in MC and FRQ every year; sign errors between sin and cos antiderivatives are the most common distractor.

in_depth.label: Antiderivative of e^x and Exponential Functions
in_depth.description: Applies ∫e^x dx = e^x + C and ∫e^{kx} dx = (1/k)e^{kx} + C; distinguishes e^x (derivative = itself) from other exponential bases.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Exponential antiderivatives appear in integration and DE sub-parts; forgetting the 1/k factor is a classic distractor.

in_depth.label: Antiderivative of 1/x
in_depth.description: Applies ∫(1/x) dx = ln|x| + C; recognizes when the integrand has the form 1/x (or f'(x)/f(x)) and selects the logarithmic antiderivative; includes the absolute value in the argument.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reciprocal antiderivative (ln|x|) is tested in MC and appears in integrate_log_form problems; the absolute value requirement is a common error.

in_depth.label: Constant Multiple and Sum/Difference Rules for Antiderivatives
in_depth.description: Applies ∫[cf(x) ± g(x)] dx = c∫f(x) dx ± ∫g(x) dx to integrate term-by-term; distributes the constant multiple before integrating each term.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Term-by-term integration of polynomials and mixed functions is required in every antiderivative computation on the exam.

in_depth.label: Including and Interpreting the Constant of Integration +C
in_depth.description: Recognizes that every indefinite integral requires +C; understands that omitting C loses points on FRQ; distinguishes indefinite integrals (family of functions) from definite integrals (a number).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Missing +C is an explicit FRQ point deduction; tested in MC via 'which expression is an antiderivative of...' format.

umbrellas.label: Integration by U-Substitution
umbrellas.description: Transforms indefinite and definite integrals by substituting u = g(x) and du = g'(x) dx; recognizes when the integrand contains a function and its derivative; adjusts limits of integration for definite u-sub.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: U-substitution is the primary integration technique on the AB exam; appears in MC and as a FRQ sub-step every year.

in_depth.label: Recognizing When U-Substitution Applies
in_depth.description: Identifies that u-substitution is needed when the integrand contains a composite function whose inner derivative (or a constant multiple thereof) is also present; chooses the correct u = g(x).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Pattern recognition is the first step that separates students who can do u-sub from those who cannot; tested via MC 'which substitution works here?' questions.

in_depth.label: Computing du and Rewriting the Integral in Terms of u
in_depth.description: Differentiates u = g(x) to get du = g'(x) dx; algebraically isolates dx or adjusts a constant coefficient so the integral is fully expressed in terms of u and du.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Correct du computation and substitution is the execution step of every u-sub problem; errors here are the most common source of wrong answers.

in_depth.label: Completing an Indefinite U-Substitution and Back-Substituting
in_depth.description: Integrates ∫f(u) du using basic rules after substitution; back-substitutes u = g(x) to express the antiderivative in terms of the original variable; adds +C.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Full indefinite u-sub is tested in MC and required in FRQ; forgetting to back-substitute is an explicit error.

in_depth.label: U-Substitution in a Definite Integral — Changing Limits
in_depth.description: Converts the limits of integration from x-values to u-values when applying u-sub to a definite integral; evaluates the integral entirely in terms of u without back-substituting.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Definite u-sub with updated bounds appears in MC and no-calc FRQ; failing to update bounds while leaving a definite integral is the primary error.

in_depth.label: U-Substitution with Trigonometric Integrands
in_depth.description: Applies u-substitution to integrands of the form sin(g(x))·g'(x) or cos(g(x))·g'(x); uses the standard trig antiderivatives after substituting.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Trig composite integrals via u-sub appear in MC; arctan integral (1/(1+u²)) also arises in this context.

in_depth.label: Adjusting a Constant Factor to Complete U-Substitution
in_depth.description: Handles the case where g'(x) is off by a constant factor (e.g., integrand has 2x but u = x², so du = 2x dx matches); multiplies or divides by the appropriate constant to complete the substitution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Constant-factor adjustment in u-sub is tested in MC; students who only recognize 'exact' matches miss these problems.

umbrellas.label: Algebraic Rewriting for Integration: Long Division and Completing the Square
umbrellas.description: Rewrites improper rational integrands via polynomial long division or completes the square in a denominator to transform integrands into integrable forms; selects the appropriate algebraic technique before integrating.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Less frequently tested directly; appears in MC roughly every 2–3 years; important as a technique selection skill but not a core FRQ archetype.

in_depth.label: Polynomial Long Division to Prepare an Integral
in_depth.description: Performs polynomial long division on an improper rational integrand (degree of numerator ≥ degree of denominator) to rewrite it as a polynomial plus a proper rational remainder; then integrates each piece using basic rules.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Long division for integration is tested in MC every 2–3 years; the algebraic step itself is the point of difficulty.

in_depth.label: Completing the Square in a Denominator to Enable Integration
in_depth.description: Rewrites a quadratic expression in the denominator by completing the square to obtain the form (x − h)² + k²; enables a u-substitution that yields an arctan antiderivative.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Completing-the-square integration appears occasionally in MC; lower frequency but tests combined algebraic and integration fluency.

in_depth.label: Identifying When an Integrand Requires Algebraic Rewriting
in_depth.description: Determines that a given rational or quadratic integrand cannot be integrated directly; selects long division (improper rational) or completing the square (sum-of-squares denominator) as the appropriate first step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Technique-selection for rewriting is tested as the 6.14 strategy topic; students who skip the rewriting step produce incorrect integrals.

in_depth.label: Integrating Rational Expressions That Yield ln|x|
in_depth.description: Recognizes integrands of the form k/x or f'(x)/f(x) and applies the logarithmic antiderivative; distinguishes this case from the power rule (n = −1 exception).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rational integrals yielding ln appear in u-sub and direct integration MC questions; the n = −1 exception to the power rule is a targeted misconception.

umbrellas.label: Selecting Techniques for Antidifferentiation
umbrellas.description: Surveys a given integrand and selects the appropriate technique — direct antiderivative, u-substitution, or algebraic manipulation — before executing; includes recognizing when an integrand in a non-standard form must be rewritten first.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Strategy-selection MC questions test this explicitly; unit 17–20% weighting but topic contributes via guiding all integration computations.

in_depth.label: Choosing Between Direct Antiderivative and U-Substitution
in_depth.description: Distinguishes integrands that match a basic formula directly (e.g., ∫x² dx) from those requiring u-substitution (e.g., ∫x·cos(x²) dx); selects the correct first move.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Strategy questions appear in MC; the direct vs. u-sub decision is where students make the most technique errors.

in_depth.label: Identifying a Composite Structure That Triggers U-Substitution
in_depth.description: Spots the f(g(x))·g'(x) pattern in an integrand and names the inner function u = g(x); distinguishes it from a product that requires a different approach.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Composite pattern recognition is the key skill for u-sub; tested implicitly on every u-sub question and explicitly in technique-selection MC.

in_depth.label: Recognizing When Algebraic Manipulation Must Precede Integration
in_depth.description: Identifies integrands that must be expanded, simplified, or rewritten (e.g., (x + 1)² expanded before power rule; (x² + 1)/x split into x + 1/x) before any integration rule can be applied.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Algebraic preprocessing before integrating appears in MC; students who jump to u-sub on an expandable expression waste effort and introduce errors.

in_depth.label: Executing a Multi-Step Integration Plan
in_depth.description: Chains two or more steps (e.g., long division then power rule; completing the square then u-sub) to evaluate an integrand that does not yield to a single technique; demonstrates technique fluency in sequence.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multi-step integration appears in harder MC and FRQ bonus sub-parts; tests both strategy and execution fluency.
